summary: Gruber criticizes Twitter's decision to break functionality for third-party clients, arguing that users prefer apps like Tweetbot because they present tweets chronologically and respect what Twitter fundamentally is: a real-time information stream. He argues Twitter should reverse course, replace deprecated APIs with new ones, and find ways to monetize third-party client usage rather than killing it. He draws an analogy to Apple's iOS platform, where default apps coexist with third-party alternatives, and notes the irony that the Twitter executive defending the policy uses a third-party email client on his own iPhone.
key_points: Third-party clients like Tweetbot succeed because they present Twitter as a chronological real-time stream rather than an algorithmic feed
Twitter should treat itself as a platform and let third-party clients flourish, monetizing them through required ads or user fees
Cutting off third-party clients is analogous to Apple killing third-party iOS apps for maps, email, and calendar — a foolish move for a platform
Twitter's own executive defending the policy ironically uses a third-party email client, undermining his argument that one size fits all
money_quotes: I truly find it confusing. And Twitter no longer even fucking *has* a first-party native app for the Mac.
I don't want to use a website for Twitter. I want an app.
I am convinced there are ways Twitter could make money from people using third-party clients. I am equally convinced that there's no way Twitter can make one interface that pleases all of its users.
Twitter cutting off third-party clients is as foolish and wrongheaded as it would be if Apple cut off third-party iOS apps for maps, email, calendar, notes, podcasts, music, etc.
I prefer a third-party Twitter client for the same reason you prefer a third-party iOS email client. One size doesn't fit all.
Twitter has always been about a real-time stream of information. It's not meant to be another Facebook-style algorithmic News Feed.
key_insight: Twitter's hostility toward third-party clients reveals a fundamental misunderstanding of its own nature as a platform, where one interface will never satisfy all users.
